steps.text: You learned to choose scales. You checked if the biggest number fits. You checked the last digits. Now let's connect what you discovered.

condition.selected: Do the numbers end in 0 or 5?

incorrects.description: Student selected checking digits first instead of checking if biggest number fits

steps.text: Let's think about this together. If the biggest number doesn't fit, the scale won't work at all. Check that first.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Scale Preview System highlights the maximum value check, showing 23 fits on Scale of 5

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. First, check if the biggest number fits. Here, 23 fits on Scale of 5. If it didn't fit, that scale wouldn't work at all. After you know it fits, then you check if the numbers end in 0 or 5.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale on first attempt

steps.text: Look closely. Which graph has the fewest lines on the side?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale on second attempt

params.description: Scale of 10 graph highlights to show fewer tick marks.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Count the lines on each graph. The one with the fewest lines is cleaner and easier to read quickly.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong scale three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Scale of 10 graph animates to show it is the correct answer.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Scale of 10 has the fewest lines. That makes it easiest to read. When your data uses big numbers like 20, 40, 60, a bigger scale keeps the graph clean. Click Scale of 10.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong table on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Check which numbers can't be shown with Scale of 5 or 10.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong table on second attempt

params.description: Coins table highlights to draw attention.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Look at the coin counts: 7, 12, 19, 23. None of these end in 0 or 5, so you need Scale of 2.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong table three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Each coin value highlights in sequence: 7, 12, 19, 23.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. I check each number: 7 doesn't end in 0 or 5. 12 doesn't. 19 doesn't. 23 doesn't. So the coins HAVE to use Scale of 2. Click the coins table.

params.description: Vertical bar graph with Scale of 10 appears briefly. Shows completed graph.

steps.text: You learned to CHOOSE scales, not just use them. You check if the biggest number fits. You check the last digits to look for multiples of 5. Then you pick what's easiest to read without too many lines.
That's strategic thinking. You're not just following rules. You're making decisions.
Next time, you'll use your graphs to solve problems and answer questions. The scale you choose will help you find answers faster.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph with Scale of 10. Shows completed graph with three categories.
